capstone-storage-and-reports.label: Capstone Storage and Reports
capstone-storage-and-reports.summary: Load task data into objects and turn those objects into useful report output.

try_capstone_storage_and_reports_sketch0.title: Load task objects from CSV
try_capstone_storage_and_reports_sketch0.prompt: Complete storage/task_storage.py so load_tasks(path) reads data/tasks.csv and returns the correct task subclasses in file order. The supplied runner prints the count and first title.
try_capstone_storage_and_reports_sketch0.hint: Use DictReader, convert completed to bool, and convert feature detail to int.

help.concept: Storage code translates serialized rows into validated domain objects.
help.hint_1: Use DictReader, convert completed to bool, and convert feature detail to int.
help.hint_2: Use the requested file boundary as part of the solution contract.

try_capstone_storage_and_reports_sketch0.starterCode:
# Run this provided storage smoke test.
from storage.task_storage import load_tasks

tasks = load_tasks("data/tasks.csv")
print(len(tasks))
print(tasks[0].title)

0.message: Load three tasks and preserve the first title.

capstone-storage-and-reports-sketch-0.title: Capstone storage and reports: turn objects into useful output
capstone-storage-and-reports-sketch-0.bodyMarkdown:
Storage and reports are where an OOP project starts to feel like an app.

Use a different mental model first: a pet-sitting app might load pet visits from a CSV file, create `Visit` objects, and print a daily report. The report should not care about raw CSV rows; it should work with objects.

The Smart Task Tracker follows that same pattern:

1. Read raw data.
2. Create task objects.
3. Let services organize those objects.
4. Print or save a report.

Keep the boundary clear: loading turns text into objects; reporting turns objects into readable output.

Storage should translate between CSV rows and domain objects without teaching the model about filenames. Reports should consume the domain contract so the same objects can be displayed, saved, or tested consistently.
